Title: How scent and nectar influence floral antagonists and mutualists

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: WT line from DI Ranch, Santa Clara, UT, inbred for 22 or 30 generations
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: EV line A-04-266-3; SWEET9 line A-10-198-4; CHALxSWEET9 cross
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Corolla tube/floral nectar of newly opened flowers
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Rewards pollinators, promotes outcrossing, and increases oviposition by the hawkmoth herbivore Manduca sexta
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing SWEET9 eliminates floral nectar production, markedly reduces pollination success for several pollinators, and strongly reduces M. sexta oviposition.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Flower-associated; functional effect assayed in nectar secretion/accumulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi silencing (pSOL8SWEET9); nectar volume measurements; field pollination assays; tent single-pollinator assays; oviposition assays
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field conditions in Utah native habitat; tent assays; nectar measured in newly opened flowers between 5 and 6 am
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: In hemizygous CHALxSWEET9 plants, occasional late-stage recovery of nectar secretion was attributed by the authors to methylation events in aging hemizygous lines.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of nectar lowers outcrossing and seed set, but strongly lowers M. sexta oviposition; nectar creates a trade-off between pollination benefit and herbivory cost
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing nectar via SWEET9 could reduce herbivore oviposition, but with substantial risk of reduced pollination services; useful only where selfing or alternative pollinators can compensate
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Native populations contain rare heritable nectar-free phenotypes. 2. RNAi silencing of SWEET9 abolished nectar production in N. attenuata flowers. 3. SWEET9-silenced plants had strongly reduced seed set from pollinator-mediated outcrossing in field and species-specific pollination assays. 4. SWEET9 silencing reduced M. sexta oviposition more strongly than CHAL silencing, indicating nectar has major influence on host choice.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This paper studies SWEET9 as a determinant of nectar production rather than a classical biosynthetic enzyme or transcriptional regulator; therefore classified as Common_Gene. Nectar sugar concentration was largely unchanged among transformed lines; the major effect was on nectar volume/absence.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: WT line from DI Ranch, Santa Clara, UT, inbred for 22 or 30 generations
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: EV line A-04-266-3; CHAL line A-07-283-5; CHALxSWEET9 cross
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream/required step for BA production; exact step not specified in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the chalcone synthase reaction; in this study CHAL is required for floral benzylacetone emission, but the exact BA-forming reaction is not described here.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for production of the target volatile attractant
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Flower headspace/emitted from flowers at night
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Attracts pollinators and also contributes to attraction of herbivore-associated hawkmoths; mediates outcrossing and ecological interactions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing CHAL abolishes floral BA emission, strongly reduces pollinator-mediated seed set, and reduces Manduca sexta oviposition.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Flower-associated; functional effect assayed in floral scent emission at night
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type III polyketide synthase, chalcone synthase family
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for BA emission in this system, as supported by RNAi silencing phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi silencing (pRESC5CHAL); floral volatile profiling by GC-MS; field pollination assays; tent single-pollinator assays; oviposition assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field conditions in Utah native habitat; tent assays; night floral volatile emission period
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of BA decreases outcrossing, especially by Manduca sexta, but also decreases oviposition by M. sexta; potential trade-off between pollination and herbivory
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating CHAL/BA emission could tune attraction of pollinators versus herbivores, but reduced BA risks large losses in outcrossing services
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Natural BA emission varies strongly among native N. attenuata populations. 2. RNAi silencing of CHAL eliminated floral BA emission in transgenic lines. 3. CHAL-silenced plants showed much lower outcrossed seed set than controls, especially with M. sexta. 4. CHAL silencing also reduced M. sexta oviposition, revealing an ecological trade-off.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper refers to the transgenic line as CHAL and states that it silences N. attenuata chalcone synthase (also noted as Nachal1-silenced in Methods). BA emission and nectar production were uncorrelated in natural populations.
